threats.title: Generation Increment Permanently Blocked by Access Control Mismatch on initializeAlphaIndices
threats.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(), but initializeAlphaIndices has the onlyOwner modifier on EntropyGenerator. The deployment script sets TraitForgeNft as allowedCaller but does NOT transfer ownership of EntropyGenerator to TraitForgeNft. Therefore, when the 10,000th token of generation 1 is minted, _incrementGeneration reverts, permanently blocking all future minting via both mintToken and mintWithBudget. The protocol freezes at generation 1 with no further tokens mintable.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic code defect at the intersection of two contracts with mismatched access control. The deployment script confirms no ownership transfer occurs.
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: EntropyGenerator deployed with nft as constructor arg (sets allowedCaller), but no transferOwnership call is made to set TraitForgeNft as owner of EntropyGenerator.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initializeAlphaIndices() has onlyOwner modifier; TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls it as msg.sender=address(TraitForgeNft) which is not owner

preconditions: generationMintCounts[1] reaches maxTokensPerGen (10000)
EntropyGenerator owner is not TraitForgeNft (confirmed by deployment script)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TraitForgeNft constructor sets allowedCaller in EntropyGenerator, but initializeAlphaIndices requires onlyOwner not onlyAllowedCaller
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner of EntropyGenerator remains deployer EOA; TraitForgeNft is only allowedCaller
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Deterministic: occurs exactly when generation 1 fills (10000th mint)

operational_questions: Can the deployer transfer ownership of EntropyGenerator to TraitForgeNft post-deployment?
Would transferring ownership break setAllowedCaller and other owner functions?
validation_requirements: Deploy and mint 10000 tokens; verify 10000th mint reverts
Verify deployment script does not transfer EntropyGenerator ownership

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker action needed - this is a latent protocol-breaking bug
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Protocol naturally reaches 10000 mints in generation 1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All subsequent mintToken and mintWithBudget calls revert permanently

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No new tokens can ever be minted after generation 1
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete protocol freeze - minting, generation progression, and entropy cycling all halt
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeploying EntropyGenerator with correct access control or transferring ownership
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: mintToken, mintWithBudget, forge (if it triggers gen increment)

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Minting transactions reverting near generation boundary

threats.title: Emergency Pause Mechanism Completely Non-Functional Across All Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: All six contracts (TraitForgeNft, EntityForging, EntityTrading, NukeFund, EntropyGenerator, DevFund) inherit OpenZeppelin Pausable and use whenNotPaused modifiers on critical functions. However, none expose external pause() or unpause() functions. OpenZeppelin's _pause() and _unpause() are internal, requiring wrapper functions. Without them, the pause mechanism is dead code—the protocol has no emergency brake. If a vulnerability is discovered in production, the owner cannot halt operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Systematic check of all six contracts confirms no public/external pause() or unpause() function exists. The whenNotPaused guards exist but can never be activated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract inherits Pausable, uses whenNotPaused on mintToken/burn/forge, but no function calling _pause() or _unpause() exists anywhere in the contract

preconditions: Any vulnerability discovered post-deployment that requires emergency halting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pausable inherited and modifiers applied but no mechanism to toggle pause state
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner has no callable function to pause any contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - from deployment onwards, no pause is possible

operational_questions: Were pause/unpause functions intentionally omitted or accidentally forgotten?
Is there an external governance contract that could trigger pause via delegatecall?
validation_requirements: Verify no function in any contract calls _pause() or _unpause()
Attempt to call pause() on deployed contracts - confirm it fails

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker discovers exploitable vulnerability in NukeFund or EntityTrading
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol team identifies the attack but cannot pause any contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker continues exploitation unimpeded until funds are drained
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - this is a missing safety mechanism

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No emergency response capability for any contract
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Any future vulnerability has amplified impact due to inability to pause
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be fixed without redeploying all contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All whenNotPaused-gated functions across all six contracts

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Always exploitable once any vulnerability exists

threats.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Non-Functional After Generation 1 Due to Wrong Loop Variable
threats.hypothesis_description: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as its while-loop condition. _tokenIds is a global monotonically increasing counter across all generations, while maxTokensPerGen is 10,000. After generation 1 completes (10,000 tokens minted), _tokenIds >= 10000, so the loop condition is permanently false. The function silently refunds all ETH without minting any tokens for generations 2+. The correct condition should be `generationMintCounts[currentGeneration] < maxTokensPerGen`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Direct code comparison: mintToken uses _mintInternal which checks generationMintCounts correctly, but mintWithBudget's loop uses the wrong variable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while (budgetLeft >= mintPrice && _tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen) — _tokenIds is global counter, not generation-specific

preconditions: Generation 1 fully minted (10000 tokens)
User attempts mintWithBudget in generation 2+

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: mintWithBudget loop never executes after gen 1; entire ETH budget is refunded
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action can fix this without contract upgrade

operational_questions: Is mintWithBudget a core user-facing function?
What percentage of mints are expected via mintWithBudget vs mintToken?
validation_requirements: Test mintWithBudget with _tokenIds > 10000; verify no tokens minted and full refund

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker action - this is a latent code defect
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After gen 1, users calling mintWithBudget get full refund without tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users must switch to mintToken (one at a time), degrading UX significantly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol minting throughput reduced, potentially affecting revenue

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Batch minting completely disabled for 90% of protocol lifetime (gens 2-10)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: mintWithBudget calls that refund entire msg.value
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: No tokens minted from mintWithBudget after gen 1

threats.title: Forging Off-by-One Allows Every Forger Token One Extra Forge Beyond Its Potential
threats.hypothesis_description: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` using inclusive comparison (<=). Since forgingCounts starts at 0 and forgePotential is the intended max, a forger with forgePotential=N can list when forgingCounts=N (N<=N passes), then forgeWithListed increments to N+1. The correct check should be `forgingCounts[tokenId] < forgePotential` (strict less-than). This allows every forger token to forge one additional time beyond its entropy-derived limit, inflating the supply of forged entities.
threats.investigation_rationale: The forger listing check (<=) and the merger check (increment-then-compare <=) create an asymmetric off-by-one. Any user can exploit this systematically.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(forgePotential > 0 && forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential) — <= allows listing when count equals potential

preconditions: Forger token exists with forgePotential > 0
Token owner lists for forging when forgingCounts equals forgePotential

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Forger at forgePotential limit can still list and complete one additional forge
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - exploitable by any forger token owner
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always available once forgingCounts reaches forgePotential

operational_questions: How many forger tokens exist with low forgePotential (1-2) where the extra forge is proportionally significant?
What is the economic impact of extra forges on entity supply?
validation_requirements: Create forger with forgePotential=1, forge twice, verify second forge succeeds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Own a forger token with forgePotential=N
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call listForForging — check N <= N passes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Another user calls forgeWithListed — forgingCounts becomes N+1, exceeding intended limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Need a merger token owner willing to forge (or self-own both tokens via different addresses)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inflated entity supply undermines rarity and forging economy
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot retroactively fix forged entities; requires code update

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - call listForForging at the right time

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Successful forging at the forgePotential boundary
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users listing forger tokens at their count limit

threats.title: Excess ETH Permanently Locked in EntityForging Contract with No Recovery Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed checks `require(msg.value >= forgingFee)` but does not refund excess ETH. Unlike mintToken which refunds overpayment, forgeWithListed distributes only `forgingFee` (split between nukeFund and forger owner). Any ETH above forgingFee remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract, which has no receive(), fallback(), or withdrawal function.
threats.investigation_rationale: Direct code comparison with mintToken (which refunds excess) reveals the inconsistency. EntityForging has no ETH recovery mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) — excess not refunded; devFee and forgerShare calculated from forgingFee only, not msg.value

preconditions: User sends msg.value > forgingFee when calling forgeWithListed

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH accumulates in EntityForging contract balance with no extraction path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to withdraw trapped ETH
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every forging transaction where msg.value exceeds exact fee

operational_questions: What is the expected frequency of overpayment in forging transactions?
How much ETH could accumulate over protocol lifetime?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH in forgeWithListed; verify contract balance increases and no refund sent
Verify no withdrawal function exists in EntityForging

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User accidentally or unknowingly sends more ETH than required
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Only forgingFee amount is distributed (devFee + forgerShare)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Excess ETH remains in EntityForging contract permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are irrecoverable with no sweep function
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - happens naturally with imprecise user payments

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cumulative loss of user funds over time

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero ETH balance in EntityForging contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract balance growing without any distribution path

threats.title: Generation Counter Can Exceed maxGeneration Allowing Unauthorized Minting Beyond Design Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: _incrementGeneration increments currentGeneration without checking if it would exceed maxGeneration. When generation 10 (maxGeneration) fills up, _incrementGeneration sets currentGeneration to 11. Subsequent mints create tokens in generation 11 which violates the maxGeneration=10 cap. The forge() function has this check (newGeneration <= maxGeneration) but _mintInternal does not, creating an inconsistency. This finding is conditional on OPS-H001 (initializeAlphaIndices) being fixed.
threats.investigation_rationale: _incrementGeneration has no guard: `require(currentGeneration < maxGeneration)` is absent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _incrementGeneration does currentGeneration++ without checking currentGeneration < maxGeneration; no upper bound enforcement

preconditions: OPS-H001 (initializeAlphaIndices access control) must be fixed first
All 10 generations (100,000 tokens) must be fully minted
currentGeneration == maxGeneration when the last token triggers _incrementGeneration

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No maxGeneration bound check in _incrementGeneration; generation counter is uncapped
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Cannot be mitigated without code change
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When generation 10 fills (10000th mint in gen 10)

operational_questions: Should minting stop entirely after maxGeneration, or should it just stop incrementing?
Does exceeding maxGeneration break other protocol assumptions?
validation_requirements: With fixed H001, mint all 100000 tokens and verify gen 11 tokens are created

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for (or accelerate) minting to fill generation 10
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Continue minting in generation 11 beyond the intended 10-generation cap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Infinite token supply becomes possible if no other checks intervene

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token supply exceeds design maximum, diluting NukeFund per-token value
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token supply integrity, pricing model, NukeFund economics

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Continued minting after expected protocol end

threats.title: Uninitialized ageMultiplier Nullifies Time-Based Nuke Value Accrual Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.ageMultiplier defaults to 0 and is never set in the deployment script. calculateAge multiplies by ageMultiplier, so all age calculations return 0 regardless of hold time. This means calculateNukeFactor depends solely on initialNukeFactor (entropy/40), completely negating the time-based hold-to-earn mechanism. Users can nuke immediately after minimumDaysHeld (3 days) and receive the same payout as if they held for years. High-entropy tokens (especially 999999 alpha: initialNukeFactor=24999) can claim ~25% of the NukeFund without extended holding.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script (deployTasks.ts) never calls setAgeMultplier(). The constructor doesn't initialize it. Default uint256 = 0.
deployment_assumptions: ageMultiplier must be set post-deployment via setAgeMultplier() but deployment script omits this call
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: deploy-all task does not call nukeFund.setAgeMultplier(); NukeFund constructor does not set ageMultiplier to a non-zero value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: calculateAge: age = (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * ageMultiplier) / 365 — when ageMultiplier=0, age is always 0

preconditions: ageMultiplier == 0 (default, confirmed not set in deployment)
User has a high-entropy token
minimumDaysHeld (3 days) has passed since last transfer

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: NukeFund payout based purely on entropy, not hold time; contradicts documented hold-to-earn design
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to call setAgeMultplier() post-deployment — deployment script omits this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until admin manually sets ageMultiplier

operational_questions: Is there a separate deployment/setup script that sets ageMultiplier?
What is the intended ageMultiplier value?
validation_requirements: Check ageMultiplier on deployed contract (should be 0)
Calculate nuke payout for high-entropy token and verify age has no effect

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Mint or acquire high-entropy token (e.g., entropy near 999999)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Approve NukeFund and call nuke() — payout = fund * (entropy/40) / 100000
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: For entropy=999999: claim ~25% of entire NukeFund immediately

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Early high-entropy token holders can drain NukeFund disproportionately
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Setting ageMultiplier later doesn't retroactively fix early payouts

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - understand which tokens have high entropy

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Quick nuke after minimum hold period with high payouts

threats.title: Fully Predictable Entropy Enables Strategic Minting for High-Value Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: All entropy values are deterministic once written (based on block.number at batch write time) and publicly readable via getPublicEntropy(). The sequential consumption via getNextEntropy (using public currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex) means any user can predict exactly which entropy value the next minted token will receive. An attacker can monitor on-chain state, wait for a high-value entropy (high nukeFactor, 999999 alpha, high forgePotential) to be next in sequence, and time their mint to capture it. This completely undermines trait fairness.
threats.investigation_rationale: getPublicEntropy is public view; currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex are public state variables; entropy derivation is deterministic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPublicEntropy is public view returning entropy for any slot/number; currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex are public; next entropy is fully predictable

preconditions: Entropy batches have been written (confirmed in deployment script)
Attacker can read on-chain state (currentSlotIndex, currentNumberIndex)
No other user mints between attacker's state read and their mint transaction

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All future token traits are publicly predictable from on-chain data
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action can fix predictability without contract redesign

operational_questions: How many high-value entropy positions exist in the 10010-value sequence?
What is the economic value of capturing a 999999 alpha token?
validation_requirements: Call getPublicEntropy for all slots and enumerate high-value positions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Read all 770 entropy slots via getPublicEntropy — map the complete entropy sequence
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Identify positions with high entropy values (999999, high nukeFactor, high forgePotential)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Monitor currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex to track sequence consumption
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When next entropy is high-value, submit mintToken to capture it
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single actor with on-chain monitoring script

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fairness of trait distribution completely compromised
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Fundamental design issue requiring VRF or commit-reveal scheme
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All minting, forging economy, nuke fund payouts

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - must build entropy sequence map and monitoring bot
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When desirable entropy value is next in sequence

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Mints that consistently capture high-value entropies
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Single address owning disproportionate number of high-entropy tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Minting pattern that skips low-value entropies

threats.title: Entropy Index Wrap-Around Causes Identical Token Traits Across Generations
threats.hypothesis_description: EntropyGenerator stores 770 slots × 13 values = 10,010 entropy values. After all are consumed, getNextEntropy wraps currentSlotIndex back to 0. With 10,000 tokens per generation and up to 10 generations (100,000 tokens), entropy values are reused approximately 10 times. _incrementGeneration calls initializeAlphaIndices (resetting the 999999 position) but does NOT reinitialize entropy slot data. Tokens in generation 2+ receive identical entropy values as generation 1 tokens, making their traits, forgePotential, and nukeFactor completely predictable.
threats.investigation_rationale: getNextEntropy wraps at maxSlotIndex-1; writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 can only be called once each; no mechanism to regenerate entropy data per generation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getNextEntropy wraps currentSlotIndex to 0 when >= maxSlotIndex-1; entropy slot data is write-once (batch functions have require guards preventing re-initialization)

preconditions: More than 10,010 tokens minted across all generations
Entropy batches already fully written (confirmed in deployment script)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After 10010 mints, entropy sequence repeats; generation 2+ tokens have identical traits to generation 1
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to re-randomize entropy slots
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After first 10010 tokens are minted (start of gen 2)

operational_questions: Does the 10-token offset (10010 vs 10000) cause slot misalignment across generations?
Are generation 2+ economics affected by known-duplicate traits?
validation_requirements: Mint 10011 tokens; verify token 10011 has same entropy as token 1

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Map entire entropy sequence in generation 1 (publicly readable)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: In generation 2+, know exact traits of every future token based on position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Strategically mint high-value positions; avoid low-value positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Combine with OPS-H008 for complete trait prediction across all generations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single actor with entropy sequence mapping

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All trait randomness is eliminated for generation 2+
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Fundamental design issue - entropy batch functions are one-time only
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Trait fairness, rarity system, game mechanics for gens 2-10

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Duplicate entropy values between gen 1 and gen 2+ tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Strategic minting targeting known high-value positions

threats.title: Forger ForgePotential TOCTOU: Year Reset Between Listing and Forging Bypasses Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed does not re-validate the forger's forgePotential at forge time. It only increments forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] without checking against forgePotential. The check was done at listing time in listForForging, but _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called in forgeWithListed which can reset forgingCounts to 0 if a year has passed. This creates a TOCTOU: forger lists at count=forgePotential (allowed by off-by-one), a year passes, forgeWithListed resets count to 0 and increments to 1. The forger can repeat this cycle indefinitely, forging unlimited times with a single listing per year cycle.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comment in forgeWithListed says 'already checked in listForForging' but the state can change between listing and forging due to year reset.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without re-checking against forgePotential; preceded by _resetForgingCountIfNeeded which may reset count to 0

preconditions: Forger token is listed for forging
One year (365 days by default) passes between listing and forging
A merger token owner executes forgeWithListed

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Listing persists indefinitely; forgePotential check at listing time becomes stale after year reset
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Each year boundary creates new forge opportunities for stale listings

operational_questions: Do listings expire or have a time-to-live?
How common are listings that persist across year boundaries?
validation_requirements: List forger at max count, wait 1 year, forge — verify reset enables additional forge

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Own forger token, exhaust forgePotential, list at max count
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Have merger call forgeWithListed — _resetForgingCountIfNeeded resets to 0, then increments to 1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat annually for unlimited forges from a single token
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Need merger partner; 1 year wait per cycle

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Forging limits become meaningless over time
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code update to add forge-time validation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Forging count resets for listed tokens
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token forging well beyond its forgePotential over multiple years
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Annual forging patterns from long-lived listings

threats.title: taxCut=0 Causes Division-by-Zero DoS Across All Economic Functions
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.receive(), EntityForging.forgeWithListed(), and EntityTrading.buyNFT() all use taxCut as a divisor (e.g., `devShare = msg.value / taxCut`). The setTaxCut() functions in all three contracts have no validation that _taxCut > 0. If any owner sets taxCut to 0, all transactions that send ETH through that contract will revert with a division-by-zero error. For NukeFund, this blocks all minting (since _distributeFunds sends ETH to NukeFund), all trading, and all forging — a complete protocol halt.
threats.investigation_rationale: Three independent setTaxCut functions with no minimum value validation; taxCut used as divisor in critical ETH distribution paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 devShare = msg.value / taxCut; — no guard against taxCut == 0; setTaxCut has no require(_taxCut > 0)

preconditions: Owner calls setTaxCut(0) on NukeFund, EntityForging, or EntityTrading

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No input validation on setTaxCut; value directly used as divisor

operational_questions: Is there governance process or multisig preventing accidental setTaxCut(0)?
Would setting taxCut to 1 also cause issues (100% to dev, 0% to fund)?
validation_requirements: Call setTaxCut(0) then attempt mint/trade/forge — verify division by zero revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All subsequent ETH sent to NukeFund reverts (division by zero in receive())
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All minting halts (_distributeFunds sends to NukeFund which reverts)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All trading and forging also halt if their taxCut is set to 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Owner key compromise or malicious admin

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: No minting, trading, forging, or nuke fund deposits possible
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can call setTaxCut(10) to restore; requires owner action

threats.title: NukeFund Claim Cap Bypassable When potentialClaimAmount Exceeds maxAllowedClaimAmount at Default Settings
threats.hypothesis_description: The nuke() claim cap only activates when `finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam` (default 50000). The potentialClaimAmount = fund * finalNukeFactor / MAX_DENOMINATOR is used directly when finalNukeFactor <= nukeFactorMaxParam. At default settings (nukeFactorMaxParam = 50000, maxAllowedClaimDivisor = 2), this is consistent: max uncapped claim = fund * 50000 / 100000 = 50% = fund / 2. However, if the owner sets nukeFactorMaxParam higher (e.g., 80000) without adjusting maxAllowedClaimDivisor, the uncapped window expands: a token with finalNukeFactor = 79999 would claim fund * 79999 / 100000 ≈ 80% of the fund, bypassing the intended 50% cap. Additionally, if maxAllowedClaimDivisor is set to 1, the cap becomes fund/1 = 100% — a single nuke drains the entire fund.
threats.investigation_rationale: The cap mechanism relies on consistent parameter relationships, but setNukeFactorMaxParam and setMaxAllowedClaimDivisor have no cross-validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimAmount = finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam ? maxAllowedClaimAmount : potentialClaimAmount — no validation that potentialClaimAmount <= maxAllowedClaimAmount in the else branch

preconditions: Owner sets nukeFactorMaxParam > MAX_DENOMINATOR / maxAllowedClaimDivisor (parameter inconsistency)
OR owner sets maxAllowedClaimDivisor to 1
Token exists with finalNukeFactor between old and new nukeFactorMaxParam

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Claim cap depends on parameter consistency between nukeFactorMaxParam and maxAllowedClaimDivisor but no enforcement exists
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to set inconsistent parameters

operational_questions: What is the intended relationship between nukeFactorMaxParam and maxAllowedClaimDivisor?
Are parameter changes governed by timelock or multisig?
validation_requirements: Set nukeFactorMaxParam to 80000, verify nuke claims up to 80% of fund
Set maxAllowedClaimDivisor to 0, verify division by zero

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker holds high-entropy aged token with finalNukeFactor = 90000
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Cap not triggered (90000 <= 99999), attacker drains 90% in single nuke

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Near-total NukeFund drainage in single transaction
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fund depleted for remaining NFT holders

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setNukeFactorMaxParam or setMaxAllowedClaimDivisor calls with abnormal values
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Parameter change followed by large nuke claim
